Malay](/wiki/Tenggarong_Kutai_Malay "Tenggarong Kutai Malay")

([UK](/wiki/British_English "British English"): [/məˈleɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*mə-LAY*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");[[9]](#cite_note-9) [endonym](/wiki/Endonym "Endonym"): *Bahasa Melayu*, [Jawi script](/wiki/Jawi_script "Jawi script"): بهاس ملايو) is an [Austronesian language](/wiki/Austronesian_languages "Austronesian languages") spoken primarily in several islands of Maritime Southeast Asia and [Malay Peninsula](/wiki/Malay_Peninsula "Malay Peninsula") on mainland Asia.[[10]](#cite_note-Lowenberg-10) The language is an [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") of [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") and [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"). [Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language"), a standardized variety of Malay,[[3]](#cite_note-bc-3) is the official language of [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") and one of the working languages of [Timor-Leste](/wiki/Timor-Leste "Timor-Leste"). Malay is also spoken as a regional language of [ethnic Malays](/wiki/Malays_(ethnic_group) "Malays (ethnic group)") in [Indonesia](/wiki/Malay_Indonesians "Malay Indonesians"), southeast [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") and the [southern part](/wiki/Thai_Malays "Thai Malays") of [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"). Altogether, it is spoken by 60 million people across [Maritime Southeast Asia](/wiki/Maritime_Southeast_Asia "Maritime Southeast Asia").[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Austronesian language

Not to be confused with [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam"). This article is about the language on which standard Indonesian and standard Malay are based. For the standard Malay used in Brunei, Malaysia, and Singapore also called "Malay", see [Malaysian Malay](/wiki/Malaysian_Malay "Malaysian Malay"). For the vernacular varieties and dialects of Malay, see [Malayic languages](/wiki/Malayic_languages "Malayic languages"). For other uses, see [Malay](/wiki/Malay_(disambiguation) "Malay (disambiguation)").

**Malay** ([UK](/wiki/British_English "British English"): [/məˈleɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*mə-LAY*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");[[9]](#cite_note-9) [endonym](/wiki/Endonym "Endonym"): *Bahasa Melayu*, [Jawi script](/wiki/Jawi_script "Jawi script"): بهاس ملايو) is an [Austronesian language](/wiki/Austronesian_languages "Austronesian languages") spoken primarily in several islands of Maritime Southeast Asia and [Malay Peninsula](/wiki/Malay_Peninsula "Malay Peninsula") on mainland Asia.[[10]](#cite_note-Lowenberg-10) The language is an [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") of [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") and [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"). [Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language"), a standardized variety of Malay,[[3]](#cite_note-bc-3) is the official language of [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") and one of the working languages of [Timor-Leste](/wiki/Timor-Leste "Timor-Leste"). Malay is also spoken as a regional language of [ethnic Malays](/wiki/Malays_(ethnic_group) "Malays (ethnic group)") in [Indonesia](/wiki/Malay_Indonesians "Malay Indonesians"), southeast [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") and the [southern part](/wiki/Thai_Malays "Thai Malays") of [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"). Altogether, it is spoken by 60 million people across [Maritime Southeast Asia](/wiki/Maritime_Southeast_Asia "Maritime Southeast Asia").[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The language is [pluricentric](/wiki/Pluricentric "Pluricentric") and a [macrolanguage](/wiki/ISO_639_macrolanguage "ISO 639 macrolanguage"), i.e., a group of [mutually intelligible](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") speech varieties, or [dialect continuum](/wiki/Dialect_continuum "Dialect continuum"), that have no traditional name in common, and which may be considered distinct languages by their speakers. Several varieties of it are standardized as the national language (*bahasa kebangsaan* or *bahasa nasional*) of several nation states with various official names: in Malaysia, it is designated as either *Bahasa Melayu* ("Malay language") or in some instances, *Bahasa Malaysia* ("Malaysian language");[[13]](#cite_note-:1-13) in Singapore and Brunei, it is called *Bahasa Melayu* ("Malay language") where it in the latter country refers to a formal standard variety set apart from its own [vernacular dialect](/wiki/Brunei_Malay "Brunei Malay");[[a]](#cite_note-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15) in Indonesia, an autonomous normative variety called *Bahasa Indonesia* ("[Indonesian language](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language")") is designated the *bahasa persatuan/pemersatu* ("unifying language" or [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")) whereas the term "Malay" (*bahasa Melayu*) refers to vernacular varieties of Malay [indigenous](/wiki/Malay_Indonesians "Malay Indonesians") to areas of Central to Southern [Sumatra](/wiki/Sumatra "Sumatra") and [West Kalimantan](/wiki/West_Kalimantan "West Kalimantan") as the ethnic languages of [Malay](/wiki/Malay_Indonesians "Malay Indonesians") in Indonesia.[[13]](#cite_note-:1-13)[[b]](#cite_note-18)

[Classical Malay](/wiki/Classical_Malay "Classical Malay"), also called Court Malay, was the literary standard of the pre-colonial [Malacca](/wiki/Malacca_Sultanate "Malacca Sultanate") and [Johor](/wiki/Johor_Sultanate "Johor Sultanate") Sultanates and so the language is sometimes called Malacca, Johor or Riau Malay (or various combinations of those names) to distinguish it from the various other [Malayic languages](/wiki/Malayic_languages "Malayic languages"). According to *[Ethnologue](/wiki/Ethnologue "Ethnologue")* 16, several of the Malayic varieties they currently list as separate languages, including the *[Orang Asli](/wiki/Orang_Asli "Orang Asli")* varieties of the [Malay Peninsula](/wiki/Malay_Peninsula "Malay Peninsula"), are so closely related to [standard Malay](/wiki/Standard_Malay "Standard Malay") that they may prove to be dialects. There are also several [Malay trade and creole languages](/wiki/Malay_trade_and_creole_languages "Malay trade and creole languages") (e.g. [Ambonese Malay](/wiki/Ambonese_Malay "Ambonese Malay")) based on a lingua franca derived from Classical Malay as well as [Makassar Malay](/wiki/Makassar_Malay "Makassar Malay"), which appears to be a [mixed language](/wiki/Mixed_language "Mixed language").

## Origin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malay_language&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Origin")]

Malay historical linguists agree on the likelihood of the Malayic homeland being in western [Borneo](/wiki/Borneo "Borneo").[[17]](#cite_note-19) A form known as Proto-Malayic was spoken in Borneo at least by 1000 BCE, it has been argued to be the ancestral language of all subsequent [Malayic languages](/wiki/Malayic_languages "Malayic languages"). Its ancestor, [Proto-Malayo-Polynesian](/wiki/Proto-Malayo-Polynesian "Proto-Malayo-Polynesian"), a descendant of the [Proto-Austronesian language](/wiki/Proto-Austronesian_language "Proto-Austronesian language"), began to break up by at least 2000 BCE, possibly as a result of the southward expansion of [Austronesian peoples](/wiki/Austronesian_peoples "Austronesian peoples") into [Maritime Southeast Asia](/wiki/Maritime_Southeast_Asia "Maritime Southeast Asia") from the island of [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan").[[18]](#cite_note-20)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malay_language&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the Malay language](/wiki/History_of_the_Malay_language "History of the Malay language")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Srivijayan_Expansion.gif/250px-Srivijayan_Expansion.gif)](/wiki/File:Srivijayan_Expansion.gif)

Map of the expansion of the [Srivijaya](/wiki/Srivijaya "Srivijaya") empire, beginning in [Palembang](/wiki/Palembang "Palembang") in the 7th century, then extending to most of [Sumatra](/wiki/Sumatra "Sumatra"), then expanding to [Bangka Belitung](/wiki/Bangka_Belitung_Islands "Bangka Belitung Islands"), [Riau Islands](/wiki/Riau_Islands "Riau Islands"), [Malay Peninsula](/wiki/Malay_Peninsula "Malay Peninsula"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), [Java](/wiki/Java "Java"), [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"), [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia"), South [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak"), [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei"), [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah"), [West Kalimantan](/wiki/West_Kalimantan "West Kalimantan"), and ended as the [Melayu Kingdom](/wiki/Melayu_Kingdom "Melayu Kingdom") in [Jambi](/wiki/Jambi "Jambi") in the 13th century.

The history of the Malay language can be divided into five periods: Old Malay, the Transitional Period, Classical Malay, Late Modern Malay and Modern Malay. Old Malay is believed to be the actual ancestor of Classical Malay.[[19]](#cite_note-21)

Old Malay was influenced by [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit"), the ancient [Indo-Aryan language](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages") of [India](/wiki/India "India"). Sanskrit loan words can be found in Old Malay vocabulary. The earliest known stone inscription in the Old Malay language was found on the island of [Sumatra](/wiki/Sumatra "Sumatra"). Written in the [Pallava variety](/wiki/Pallava_alphabet "Pallava alphabet") of the [Grantha alphabet](/wiki/Grantha_alphabet "Grantha alphabet"),[[20]](#cite_note-22) it is dated 1 May 683. Known as the [Kedukan Bukit inscription](/wiki/Kedukan_Bukit_inscription "Kedukan Bukit inscription"), it was discovered by the [Dutchman](/wiki/Dutch_people "Dutch people") C. J. Batenburg on 29 November 1920 at Kedukan Bukit, on the banks of the Tatang River, a tributary of the [Musi River](/wiki/Musi_River_(Indonesia) "Musi River (Indonesia)"), near [Palembang](/wiki/Palembang "Palembang"), in what is now [South Sumatra](/wiki/South_Sumatra "South Sumatra"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"). The stone measures approximately 45 by 80 centimetres (18 by 31 in). For centuries, [Srivijaya](/wiki/Srivijaya "Srivijaya"), a maritime empire based on the island from the 7th to the 11th centuries, was responsible for the spread of Old Malay throughout the [Malay Peninsula](/wiki/Malay_Peninsula "Malay Peninsula") and the [Malay Archipelago](/wiki/Malay_Archipelago "Malay Archipelago") through its expansion and economic power. Old Malay served as the [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") of traders and was widely used in various ports and marketplaces across the region.[[21]](#cite_note-23)

The Tanjung Tanah Law[[22]](#cite_note-24) was a 14th-century pre-Islamic legal text that was produced during the reign of [Adityawarman](/wiki/Adityawarman "Adityawarman") (1345–1377) of the [Melayu Kingdom](/wiki/Melayu_Kingdom "Melayu Kingdom") (also known as Malayu or Dharmasraya Kingdom), a Hindu-Buddhist kingdom that arose after the end of [Srivijayan](/wiki/Srivijaya "Srivijaya") rule in [Sumatra](/wiki/Sumatra "Sumatra"). The laws were for the [Minangkabau people](/wiki/Minangkabau_people "Minangkabau people"), who today still live in the highlands of Sumatra, [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia").

The [Terengganu Inscription Stone](/wiki/Terengganu_Inscription_Stone "Terengganu Inscription Stone") (Malay: *Batu Bersurat Terengganu*; Jawi: باتو برسورت ترڠݢانو) is a granite stele bearing an inscription in [Jawi script](/wiki/Jawi_script "Jawi script"), discovered in [Terengganu](/wiki/Terengganu "Terengganu"), on the east coast of the [Malay Peninsula](/wiki/Malay_Peninsula "Malay Peninsula") (in what is now [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia")). It is considered the earliest evidence of Classical Malay. Dated approximately to 702 [AH](/wiki/Anno_Hegirae "Anno Hegirae") (1303 [CE](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era")), it represents the oldest known evidence of Jawi writing in the [Malay world](/wiki/Malay_world "Malay world") and stands as one of the earliest testimonies to the advent of [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") as a [state religion](/wiki/State_religion "State religion") in the region. The inscription contains a proclamation issued by a ruler of Terengganu, referred to as *Seri Paduka Tuan*, urging his subjects to uphold and propagate Islam, while outlining 10 basic [Sharia laws](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia") as guidance.

Classical Malay came into widespread use as the [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") of the region during the [Malacca Sultanate](/wiki/Malacca_Sultanate "Malacca Sultanate") era (1402–1511), a powerful maritime kingdom strategically located along the [Strait of Malacca](/wiki/Strait_of_Malacca "Strait of Malacca") that became a hub of international trade and Islamic learning in the region. During this period, the Malay language developed rapidly under the influence of [Islamic literature](/wiki/Islamic_literature "Islamic literature"), which brought about significant linguistic changes, including a massive infusion of [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") vocabulary, as well as continued influence from [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") and [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language"). This enriched form of the language came to be known as Classical Malay. It was during this time the language evolved into a form recognisable to speakers of modern Malay.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

After the [Capture of Malacca](/wiki/Capture_of_Malacca_(1511) "Capture of Malacca (1511)") by the [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire") in 1511, marking the fall of the [Malacca Sultanate](/wiki/Malacca_Sultanate "Malacca Sultanate"), the [royal court](/wiki/Royal_court "Royal court") re-established itself as the [Johor Sultanate](/wiki/Johor_Sultanate "Johor Sultanate"). The court continued to use Classical Malay as its literary and administrative language. Over time, this literary tradition became strongly associated with the territories under the sultanate, including the present-day [Malaysian](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") state of [Johor](/wiki/Johor "Johor") and the [Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") province of [Riau Islands](/wiki/Riau_Islands "Riau Islands"). As a result, many assumed that the spoken Malay of Johor and Riau was closely related to Classical Malay. However, while the [literary language](/wiki/Literary_language "Literary language") used in the region reflects the classical tradition, the local spoken dialects differ.[[23]](#cite_note-25) The fall of Malacca led to the dispersal of Malay literary centres, as many literati and scholars sought refuge in areas outside the immediate control of European colonial powers. As a result, new Malay literary works began to emerge from [Aceh](/wiki/Aceh_Sultanate "Aceh Sultanate"), [Java](/wiki/Java "Java"), [Makassar](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Gowa "Sultanate of Gowa"), the [Moluccas](/wiki/Maluku_Islands "Maluku Islands"), [Champa](/wiki/Champa "Champa"), and other regions.[[24]](#cite_note-:2-26)

Among the oldest surviving letters written in Malay are the letters from Sultan Abu Hayat of [Ternate](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Ternate "Sultanate of Ternate"), in the [Maluku Islands](/wiki/Maluku_Islands "Maluku Islands") of present-day [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), dated around 1521–1522. The text is addressed to the king of [Portugal](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire"), following contact with Portuguese explorer [Francisco Serrão](/wiki/Francisco_Serr%C3%A3o "Francisco Serrão").[[25]](#cite_note-sneddon-27) The letters show a sign of non-native usage, as the Ternateans used (and still use) the unrelated [Ternate language](/wiki/Ternate_language "Ternate language"), a [West Papuan language](/wiki/West_Papuan_languages "West Papuan languages"), as their [first language](/wiki/First_language "First language"). Malay was used solely as a [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") for inter-ethnic communications.[[25]](#cite_note-sneddon-27)

The 19th century marked a period of strong [Western](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") political and commercial domination in the [Malay Peninsula](/wiki/Malay_Peninsula "Malay Peninsula") and the [Malay Archipelago](/wiki/Malay_Archipelago "Malay Archipelago"). The colonial demarcation brought by the [1824 Anglo-Dutch Treaty](/wiki/Anglo-Dutch_Treaty_of_1824 "Anglo-Dutch Treaty of 1824") led to [Dutch East India Company](/wiki/Dutch_East_India_Company "Dutch East India Company") effectively colonising the [East Indies](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies") in the south, while the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") held several colonies and protectorates in the [Malay peninsula](/wiki/British_Malaya "British Malaya") and [Borneo](/wiki/British_Borneo "British Borneo") in the north. Both colonial powers used the Malay language as a tool of centralisation and modernisation. They made use of each other's scholarly publications in developing the standardised versions of the Malay language.[[24]](#cite_note-:2-26) The flourishing of pre-modern Malay literature in the 19th century led to the rise of intellectual movements among the locals and the emergence of new communities of Malay [linguists](/wiki/Linguistics "Linguistics").

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malay_language&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Classification")]

See also: [Austronesian languages § Comparison charts](/wiki/Austronesian_languages#Comparison_charts "Austronesian languages")

Malay is a member of the [Austronesian](/wiki/Austronesian_languages "Austronesian languages") family of languages, which includes languages from [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") and the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean"), with a smaller number in continental [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"). [Malagasy](/wiki/Malagasy_language "Malagasy language"), a geographic outlier spoken in [Madagascar](/wiki/Madagascar "Madagascar") in the [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean"), is also a member of this language family. Although these languages are not necessarily mutually intelligible to any extent, their similarities are often quite apparent. In more conservative languages like Malay, many roots have come with relatively little change from their common ancestor, [Proto-Austronesian language](/wiki/Proto-Austronesian_language "Proto-Austronesian language"). There are many [cognates](/wiki/Cognate "Cognate") found in the languages' words for kinship, health, body parts and common animals. Numbers, especially, show remarkable similarities.

Within Austronesian, Malay is part of a cluster of numerous closely related [forms of speech](/wiki/Variety_(linguistics) "Variety (linguistics)") known as the [Malayic languages](/wiki/Malayic_languages "Malayic languages"), which were spread across Malaya and the Indonesian archipelago by Malay traders from Sumatra. There is disagreement as to which varieties of speech popularly called "Malay" should be considered dialects of this language, and which should be classified as distinct Malay languages. The [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") of Brunei—[Brunei Malay](/wiki/Brunei_Malay "Brunei Malay")—for example, is not readily intelligible with the [standard language](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language"), and the same is true with some lects on the Malay Peninsula such as [Kedah Malay](/wiki/Kedah_Malay "Kedah Malay"). However, both Brunei and Kedah are quite close.[[26]](#cite_note-28)

## Writing system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malay_language&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Writing system")]

Main article: [Malay alphabet](/wiki/Malay_alphabet "Malay alphabet")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Kerinci_MSS_detail.jpg/250px-Kerinci_MSS_detail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kerinci_MSS_detail.jpg)

The [Rencong alphabet](/wiki/Rencong_alphabet "Rencong alphabet"), a native writing system found in central and [South Sumatra](/wiki/South_Sumatra "South Sumatra"). The text reads (Voorhoeve's spelling): "haku manangis ma / njaru ka'u ka'u di / saru tijada da / tang [hitu hadik sa]", which is translated by Voorhoeve as: "I am weeping, calling you; though called, you do not come" (hitu adik sa- is the rest of 4th line.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/8e/KedukanBukit001.jpg)](/wiki/File:KedukanBukit001.jpg)

[Kedukan Bukit Inscription](/wiki/Kedukan_Bukit_Inscription "Kedukan Bukit Inscription"), using [Pallava alphabet](/wiki/Pallava_alphabet "Pallava alphabet"), is the oldest surviving specimen of the [Old Malay](/wiki/Old_Malay "Old Malay") language in [South Sumatra](/wiki/South_Sumatra "South Sumatra"), Indonesia.

Malay is now written using the [Latin script](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script"), known as *Rumi* in Brunei, Malaysia and Singapore or *Latin* in Indonesia, although an [Arabic script](/wiki/Arabic_script "Arabic script") called *Arab Melayu* or [*Jawi*](/wiki/Jawi_alphabet "Jawi alphabet") also exists. Latin script is official in Malaysia, Singapore, and Indonesia. Malay uses [Hindu-Arabic numerals](/wiki/Hindu-Arabic_numerals "Hindu-Arabic numerals").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Final_pages_of_the_Taj_al-Salatin%2C_The_Crown_of_Kings%2C_a_Malay_mirror_for_princes.jpg/250px-Final_pages_of_the_Taj_al-Salatin%2C_The_Crown_of_Kings%2C_a_Malay_mirror_for_princes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Final_pages_of_the_Taj_al-Salatin,_The_Crown_of_Kings,_a_Malay_mirror_for_princes.jpg)

Final pages of the Taj al-Salatin, *The Crown of Kings*, a Malay "[mirror for princes](/wiki/Mirrors_for_princes "Mirrors for princes")", copied by Muhammad bin Umar Syaikh Farid on 31 July 1824 CE in [Penang](/wiki/Penang "Penang") in Jawi script. [British Library](/wiki/British_Library "British Library")

*Rumi* (Latin) and *Jawi* are co-official in [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei") and [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") only. Names of institutions and organisations have to use Jawi and Rumi (Latin) scripts in Brunei and some parts of Malaysia. Jawi is used fully in schools, especially the religious school, *sekolah agama*, which is compulsory during the afternoon for Muslim students aged from around 6–7 up to 12–14.

Efforts are currently being undertaken to preserve Jawi in Malaysia, and students taking Malay language examinations in Malaysia have the option of answering questions using Jawi.

The Latin script, however, is the most commonly used in Brunei and Malaysia, both for official and informal purposes.

Historically, Malay has been written using various scripts. Before the introduction of Arabic script in the Malay region, Malay was written using the [Pallava](/wiki/Pallava_script "Pallava script"), [Kawi](/wiki/Kawi_script "Kawi script") and [Rencong](/wiki/Rencong_script "Rencong script") scripts; these scripts are no longer frequently used, but similar scripts such as the [Cham alphabet](/wiki/Cham_alphabet "Cham alphabet") are used by the [Chams](/wiki/Chams "Chams") of [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") and [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia"). Old Malay was written using Pallava and Kawi script, as evident from several inscription stones in the Malay region. Starting from the era of kingdom of Pasai and throughout the golden age of the Malacca Sultanate, *Jawi* gradually replaced these scripts as the most commonly used script in the Malay region. Starting from the 17th century, under [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") and British influence, Jawi was gradually replaced by the [Rumi](/wiki/Malay_alphabet "Malay alphabet") script.[[27]](#cite_note-29)

## Extent of use

The blue sign reads "*Lajur Khusus Menurunkan Penumpang*" which means "Lane for dropping passengers only" and the small no-parking sign on the left reads "*Sampai Rambu Berikutnya*" which means "until next sign" in Indonesian.

Malay is spoken in [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), [Timor-Leste](/wiki/Timor-Leste "Timor-Leste"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), southeastern [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") and southern [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand").[[28]](#cite_note-30) [Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language") is the national language in Indonesia by Article 36 of the 1945 Constitution of the Republic of Indonesia, while "Malay" (*bahasa Melayu*) has been recognised as the ethnic languages of [Malay](/wiki/Malay_Indonesians "Malay Indonesians") in Indonesia alongside [Malay-based trade and creole languages](/wiki/Malay_trade_and_creole_languages "Malay trade and creole languages") and [other ethnic languages](/wiki/Languages_of_Indonesia "Languages of Indonesia"). Malaysia and Singapore use a common standard Malay.[[29]](#cite_note-Introduction-31) Brunei, in addition to Standard Malay, uses a distinct [vernacular dialect](/wiki/Nonstandard_dialect "Nonstandard dialect") called [Brunei Malay](/wiki/Brunei_Malay "Brunei Malay"). In Timor-Leste, Indonesian is recognised by the constitution as one of two working languages (the other being [English](/wiki/English_language "English language")), alongside the official languages of [Tetum](/wiki/Tetum_language "Tetum language") and [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language").[[8]](#cite_note-easttimorgovernment.com-8) The extent to which Malay is used in these countries varies depending on historical and cultural circumstances. Malay is the national language in Malaysia by [Article 152](/wiki/Constitution_of_Malaysia#Article_152_–_National_Language_and_Other_Languages "Constitution of Malaysia") of the [Constitution of Malaysia](/wiki/Constitution_of_Malaysia "Constitution of Malaysia"), and became the sole official language in [Peninsular Malaysia](/wiki/Peninsular_Malaysia "Peninsular Malaysia") in 1968 and in [East Malaysia](/wiki/East_Malaysia "East Malaysia") gradually from 1974. [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") continues, however, to be widely used in professional and commercial fields and in the superior courts. Other minority languages are also commonly used by the country's large ethnic minorities. The situation in Brunei is similar to that in Malaysia. In the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), [Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language") is spoken by the overseas Indonesian community concentrated in [Davao City](/wiki/Davao_City "Davao City"). Functional phrases are taught to members of the [Armed Forces of the Philippines](/wiki/Armed_Forces_of_the_Philippines "Armed Forces of the Philippines") as well as local students.

Bahasa Indonesia (Indonesian) exercises in the development of Malay as an international language as well as a language of science.[[30]](#cite_note-32) The [VOA](/wiki/VOA "VOA") and [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") use Indonesian as one of their standard language for broadcasting.[[31]](#cite_note-33)[[32]](#cite_note-34) In [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), Indonesian is one of three Asian target languages, together with [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language") and [Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese"), taught in some schools as part of the [Languages Other Than English](/wiki/Languages_Other_Than_English "Languages Other Than English") programme.[[33]](#cite_note-APO-35) Indonesian has been taught in Australian schools and universities since the 1950s.[[34]](#cite_note-36) Indonesian has been recognised as an official language of the General Conference of UNESCO since 2023.[[7]](#cite_note-UNESCO-7)

### Vowels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malay_language&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Vowels")]

Malay originally had four vowels, but in many dialects today, including Standard Malay, it has six, with /i/ split into /i, e/ and /u/ split into /u, o/.[[35]](#cite_note-Clynes_and_Deterding_2011-37) Many words are commonly pronounced variably, with either [i, u] or [e, o], and relatively few words require a mid vowel [e, o].

Table of vowel phonemes of Standard Malay

|  | [Front](/wiki/Front_vowel "Front vowel") | [Central](/wiki/Central_vowel "Central vowel") | [Back](/wiki/Back_vowel "Back vowel") |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Close](/wiki/Close_vowel "Close vowel") | [i](/wiki/Close_front_unrounded_vowel "Close front unrounded vowel") |  | [u](/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel "Close back rounded vowel") |
| [Mid](/wiki/Mid_vowel "Mid vowel") | [e](/wiki/Close-mid_front_unrounded_vowel "Close-mid front unrounded vowel") | [ə](/wiki/Mid_central_vowel "Mid central vowel") | [o](/wiki/Close-mid_back_rounded_vowel "Close-mid back rounded vowel") |
| [Open](/wiki/Open_vowel "Open vowel") |  | [a](/wiki/Open_front_unrounded_vowel "Open front unrounded vowel") |  |

**Orthographic note**: both /e/ and /ə/ are written with ⟨e⟩. Orthographic /e, o/ are relatively rare, so the letter ⟨e⟩ usually represents /ə/. There are some homographs; for example, *perang* is used for both /pəraŋ/ "war" and /peraŋ ~ piraŋ/ "blond". (In Indonesia, "blond" is written as *pirang* instead of *perang*.)

Some analyses regard /ai, au, oi/ as diphthongs.[[39]](#cite_note-41)[[40]](#cite_note-42) However, [ai] and [au] can only occur in open syllables, such as *cukai* ("excise") and *pulau* ("island"). Words with a phonetic diphthong in a closed syllable, such as *baik* ("good") and *laut* ("sea"), are actually two syllables. An alternative analysis therefore treats the phonetic diphthongs [ai], [au] and [oi] as a sequence of a monophthong plus an approximant: /aj/, /aw/ and /oj/ respectively.[[41]](#cite_note-43)

There is a rule of [vowel harmony](/wiki/Vowel_harmony "Vowel harmony"): the non-open vowels /i, e, u, o/ in bisyllabic words must agree in height, so *hidung* ("nose") is allowed but \**hedung* is not.[[42]](#cite_note-44)

## Grammar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malay_language&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Grammar")]

Main article: [Malay grammar](/wiki/Malay_grammar "Malay grammar")

Malay is an [agglutinative language](/wiki/Agglutinative_language "Agglutinative language"), and new words are formed by three methods: attaching affixes onto a root word ([affixation](/wiki/Affixation "Affixation")), formation of a [compound word](/wiki/Compound_word "Compound word") (composition), or repetition of words or portions of words ([reduplication](/wiki/Reduplication "Reduplication")). Nouns and verbs may be basic roots, but frequently they are [derived](/wiki/Derivation_(linguistics) "Derivation (linguistics)") from other words by means of [prefixes](/wiki/Prefix "Prefix"), [suffixes](/wiki/Suffix "Suffix") and [circumfixes](/wiki/Circumfix "Circumfix").

Malay does not make use of [grammatical gender](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender"), and there are only a few words that use natural gender; the same word is used for 'he' and 'she' which is *dia* or for 'his' and 'her' which is *dia punya*. There is no grammatical plural in Malay either; thus *orang* may mean either 'person' or 'people'. Verbs are not [inflected](/wiki/Inflected_language "Inflected language") for person or number, and they are not marked for tense; tense is instead denoted by time adverbs (such as 'yesterday') or by other tense indicators, such as *sudah* 'already' and *belum* 'not yet'. On the other hand, there is a complex system of verb affixes to render nuances of meaning and to denote [voice](/wiki/Voice_(grammar) "Voice (grammar)") or intentional and accidental [moods](/wiki/Grammatical_mood "Grammatical mood").

Malay does not have a [grammatical subject](/wiki/Grammatical_subject "Grammatical subject") in the sense that English does. In intransitive clauses, the noun comes before the verb. When there is both an [agent](/wiki/Agent_(grammar) "Agent (grammar)") and an [object](/wiki/Object_(grammar) "Object (grammar)"), these are separated by the verb (OVA or AVO), with the difference encoded in the voice of the verb. OVA, commonly but inaccurately called "passive", is the basic and most common word order.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Main articles: [List of loanwords in Malay](/wiki/List_of_loanwords_in_Malay "List of loanwords in Malay") and [List of loanwords in Indonesian](/wiki/List_of_loanwords_in_Indonesian "List of loanwords in Indonesian")

| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Malay_language&action=edit&section=). *(July 2019)* |

The Malay language has many words borrowed from [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language") (in particular religious terms), [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit_language "Sanskrit language"), [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language"), certain [Sinitic languages](/wiki/Sinitic_languages "Sinitic languages"), [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") (due to historical status of Malay Archipelago as a trading hub), and more recently, [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"), [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") and [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") (in particular many scientific and technological terms). [Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language") has inclination toward [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") in formulation of new words due to extensive [Javanese](/wiki/Javanese_language "Javanese language") and [Balinese](/wiki/Balinese_language "Balinese language") speaking community, while Malaysian and Bruneian Malay prefer [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") as source for neologism due to acceptance of Islamic Arabic practices.[[45]](#cite_note-Omar-2008-47) Arabic in Indonesian tends to reside in (Islamic) religious sphere.[[45]](#cite_note-Omar-2008-47) The presence of Sanskritised neologism in Malaysian and Bruneian Malay is a result of "importation" from Indonesian.[[45]](#cite_note-Omar-2008-47) Terminology for various subjects such as administration, business, and law was derived from the languages of respective colonial master, those are Dutch for Indonesian and English for Malaysian and Bruneian Malay.[[45]](#cite_note-Omar-2008-47) Although the rule for scientific terms development is agreed, the result can be differ because of (1) the difference in traditional vocabulary (such as Dutch vs English and Sanskritic Javanese vs Arabised Malay) and (2) the loan-shift difference on semantics and grammatical feature choice.[[45]](#cite_note-Omar-2008-47) The divergence between Indonesian and "Standard" Malay are systemic in nature and, to a certain extent, contribute to the way the two sets of speakers understand and react to the world, and are more far reaching with a discernible cognitive gap than the difference between dialects.[[45]](#cite_note-Omar-2008-47)

## Varieties and related languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malay_language&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Varieties and related languages")]

Main articles: [Malayic languages](/wiki/Malayic_languages "Malayic languages") and [Malay trade and creole languages](/wiki/Malay_trade_and_creole_languages "Malay trade and creole languages")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Betawi.jpg/250px-Betawi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Betawi.jpg)

Mural of [Mark 1](/wiki/Mark_1 "Mark 1"):9-11 written in [Betawi language](/wiki/Betawi_language "Betawi language")

There is a group of closely related languages spoken by [Malays](/wiki/Malays_(ethnic_group) "Malays (ethnic group)") and related peoples across [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), [Southern Thailand](/wiki/Southern_Thailand "Southern Thailand"), [Timor-Leste](/wiki/Timor-Leste "Timor-Leste"), and the far southern parts of the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"). They have traditionally been classified as Malay, Para-Malay, and Aboriginal Malay, but this reflects geography and ethnicity rather than a proper linguistic classification. The Malayic languages are [mutually intelligible](/wiki/Mutually_intelligible "Mutually intelligible") to varying extents, though the distinction between language and dialect is unclear in many cases.

Para-Malay includes the Malayic languages of [Sumatra](/wiki/Sumatra "Sumatra"). They are: [Minangkabau](/wiki/Minangkabau_language "Minangkabau language"), [Central Malay](/wiki/Central_Malay "Central Malay") (Bengkulu), [Pekal](/wiki/Pekal_language "Pekal language"), Talang Mamak, [Musi](/wiki/Musi_language "Musi language") (Palembang), [Negeri Sembilan](/wiki/Negeri_Sembilan_Malay "Negeri Sembilan Malay") (Malaysia), and [Duano’](/wiki/Duano_language "Duano language").[[46]](#cite_note-48)

Aboriginal Malay are the Malayic languages spoken by the [Orang Asli](/wiki/Orang_Asli "Orang Asli") ([Proto-Malay](/wiki/Proto-Malay "Proto-Malay")) in [Malaya](/wiki/Peninsular_Malaysia "Peninsular Malaysia"). They are [Jakun](/wiki/Jakun_language "Jakun language"), [Orang Kanaq](/wiki/Orang_Kanaq_language "Orang Kanaq language"), [Orang Seletar](/wiki/Orang_Seletar_language "Orang Seletar language"), and [Temuan](/wiki/Temuan_language "Temuan language").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The other Malayic languages, included in neither of these groups, are associated with the expansion of the Malays across the archipelago. They include Riau-Johor Malay ([Malaysian](/wiki/Malaysian_language "Malaysian language") and [Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language")), [Kedah Malay](/wiki/Kedah_Malay "Kedah Malay"), [Brunei Malay](/wiki/Brunei_Malay "Brunei Malay"), [Berau Malay](/wiki/Berau_Malay "Berau Malay"), [Bangka Malay](/wiki/Bangka_Malay "Bangka Malay"), [Jambi Malay](/wiki/Jambi_Malay "Jambi Malay"), [Kutai Malay](/wiki/Kutai_Malay "Kutai Malay"), [Terengganu Malay](/wiki/Terengganu_Malay "Terengganu Malay"), Riau Malay, [Loncong](/wiki/Orang_Laut_language "Orang Laut language"), [Pattani Malay](/wiki/Pattani_Malay "Pattani Malay"), [Bacan Malay](/wiki/Bacan_Malay "Bacan Malay"), and [Banjarese](/wiki/Banjar_language "Banjar language"). [Menterap](/wiki/Menterap_language "Menterap language") may belong here.

There are also several [Malay-based creole languages](/wiki/Malay-based_creole_languages "Malay-based creole languages"), such as [Betawi Malay](/wiki/Betawi_language "Betawi language"), [Cocos Malay](/wiki/Cocos_Malay "Cocos Malay"), [Makassar Malay](/wiki/Makassar_Malay "Makassar Malay"), [Ambonese Malay](/wiki/Ambonese_Malay "Ambonese Malay"), [Dili Malay](/wiki/Dili_Malay "Dili Malay"), [Kupang Malay](/wiki/Kupang_Malay "Kupang Malay"), [Manado Malay](/wiki/Manado_Malay "Manado Malay"), [Papuan Malay](/wiki/Papuan_Malay "Papuan Malay"), [Thousand Islands Malay](/wiki/Orang_Pulo_language "Orang Pulo language"), [Larantuka Malay](/wiki/Larantuka_Malay "Larantuka Malay"), [Alor Malay](/wiki/Alor_Malay "Alor Malay"), [Balinese Malay](/wiki/Balinese_Malay "Balinese Malay"), [Sri Lankan Malay](/wiki/Sri_Lankan_Malay "Sri Lankan Malay") and [Sabah Malay](/wiki/Sabah_Malay "Sabah Malay"), which may be more or less distinct from standard (Malaccan) Malay.

Due to the early settlement of a [Cape Malay](/wiki/Cape_Malay "Cape Malay") community in [Cape Town](/wiki/Cape_Town "Cape Town"), who are now known as [Coloureds](/wiki/Coloured "Coloured"), numerous [Classical Malay](/wiki/History_of_the_Malay_language#Classical_Malay "History of the Malay language") words were brought into [Afrikaans](/wiki/Afrikaans_language "Afrikaans language").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Alamat_lankapuri_cover.png/250px-Alamat_lankapuri_cover.png)](/wiki/File:Alamat_lankapuri_cover.png)

The [Alamat Langkapuri](/wiki/Alamat_Langkapuri "Alamat Langkapuri") from [British Ceylon](/wiki/British_Ceylon "British Ceylon") (present-day [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka")). Initially published between 1869 and 1870 and written in [Jawi script](/wiki/Jawi_script "Jawi script"), it is noted to be among the first Malay-language newspaper. The readership consist of the [Malay-diaspora in Ceylon](/wiki/Sri_Lankan_Malays "Sri Lankan Malays") as well as in the Malay archipelago.

The extent to which Malay and related Malayan languages are used in the countries where it is spoken varies depending on historical and cultural circumstances. Malay is the national language in Malaysia by [Article 152](/wiki/Constitution_of_Malaysia#Article_152_–_National_Language_and_Other_Languages "Constitution of Malaysia") of the [Constitution of Malaysia](/wiki/Constitution_of_Malaysia "Constitution of Malaysia"), and became the sole official language in [West Malaysia](/wiki/West_Malaysia "West Malaysia") in 1968, and in [East Malaysia](/wiki/East_Malaysia "East Malaysia") gradually from 1974. [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") continues, however, to be widely used in professional and commercial fields and in the superior courts. Other minority languages are also commonly used by the country's large ethnic minorities. The situation in Brunei is similar to that of Malaysia.

In Singapore, Malay was historically the *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")* among people of different nationalities. Although this has largely given way to English, Malay still retains the status of national language and the [national anthem](/wiki/National_anthem "National anthem"), [Majulah Singapura](/wiki/Majulah_Singapura "Majulah Singapura"), is entirely in Malay. In addition, parade commands in the military, police and civil defence are given only in Malay.

Most residents of the five southernmost provinces of [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand")—a region that, for the most part, used to be part of an ancient Malay kingdom called [Pattani](/wiki/Pattani_kingdom "Pattani kingdom")—speak a dialect of Malay called [Yawi](/wiki/Yawi_language "Yawi language") (not to be confused with Jawi), which is similar to Kelantanese Malay, but the language has no official status or recognition.

Owing to earlier contact with the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), Malay words—such as *dalam hati* (sympathy), *luwalhati* (glory), *tengah hari* (midday), *sedap* (delicious)—have evolved and been integrated into [Tagalog](/wiki/Tagalog_language "Tagalog language") and other [Philippine languages](/wiki/Languages_of_the_Philippines "Languages of the Philippines"). This linguistic exchange reflects broader patterns of cultural interaction and mutual influence that have contributed to shared concepts of social values and inclusion within Philippine society.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Youthpledge.jpg/250px-Youthpledge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Youthpledge.jpg)

The [Youth Pledge](/wiki/Youth_Pledge "Youth Pledge") was the result of the Second Youth Congress held in [Batavia](/wiki/Batavia,_Dutch_East_Indies "Batavia, Dutch East Indies") in October 1928. On the last pledge, there was an affirmation of Indonesian language as a *unifying language* throughout the archipelago.



[Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language") speaker

By contrast, Indonesian has successfully become the *lingua franca* for its disparate islands and ethnic groups, in part because the colonial language, Dutch, is no longer commonly spoken. (In [East Timor](/wiki/East_Timor "East Timor"), which was governed as a province of Indonesia from 1976 to 1999, Indonesian is widely spoken and recognized under its [Constitution](/wiki/Constitution "Constitution") as a 'working language'.)

[Malaysian](/wiki/Malaysian_Malay "Malaysian Malay") speaker

Besides [Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesia_language "Indonesia language"), which developed from the Riau Malay dialect,[[47]](#cite_note-Melayu_Online-49) there are many Malay varieties spoken in Indonesia; they are divided into western and eastern groups. Western Malay dialects are predominantly spoken in [Sumatra](/wiki/Sumatra "Sumatra") and [Borneo](/wiki/Borneo "Borneo"), which itself is divided into Bornean and Sumatran Malay; some of the most widely spoken Sumatran Malay dialects are [Riau Malay](/wiki/Riau "Riau"), [Langkat](/wiki/Langkat "Langkat"), [Palembang Malay](/wiki/Musi_language "Musi language") and [Jambi Malay](/wiki/Jambi_Malay "Jambi Malay"). [Minangkabau](/wiki/Minangkabau_people "Minangkabau people"), [Kerinci](/wiki/Kerinci_people "Kerinci people") and [Bengkulu](/wiki/Bengkulu "Bengkulu") are believed to be Sumatran Malay descendants. Meanwhile, the [Jakarta](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta") dialect (known as [Betawi](/wiki/Betawi_language "Betawi language")) also belongs to the western Malay group.

A young man speaks Kedah Malay.

The eastern varieties, classified either as dialects or [creoles](/wiki/Malay_trade_and_creole_languages "Malay trade and creole languages"), are spoken in the eastern part of the Malay or Nusantara archipelago and include [Makassar Malay](/wiki/Makassar_Malay "Makassar Malay"), [Manado Malay](/wiki/Manado_Malay "Manado Malay"), [Ambonese Malay](/wiki/Ambonese_Malay "Ambonese Malay"), [North Moluccan Malay](/wiki/North_Moluccan_Malay "North Moluccan Malay"), [Kupang Malay](/wiki/Kupang_Malay "Kupang Malay"), [Dili Malay](/wiki/Dili_Malay "Dili Malay"), and [Papuan Malay](/wiki/Papuan_Malay "Papuan Malay").

The differences among both groups are quite observable. For example, the word *kita* means 'we, us' in western, but means 'I, me' in Manado, whereas 'we, us" in Manado is *torang* and Ambon *katong* (originally abbreviated from Malay *kita orang* 'we people'). Another difference is the lack of possessive pronouns (and suffixes) in eastern dialects. Manado uses the verb *pe* and Ambon *pu* (from Malay *punya* 'to have') to mark possession. So 'my name' and 'our house" are translated in western Malay as *namaku* and *rumah kita* but *kita pe nama* and *torang pe rumah* in Manado and *beta pu nama*, *katong pu rumah* in Ambon dialect.

The pronunciation may vary in western dialects, especially the pronunciation of words ending in the vowel 'a'. For example, in some parts of Malaysia and in Singapore, *kita* (inclusive 'we, us, our') is pronounced as /kitə/, in Kelantan and Southern Thailand as /kitɔ/, in Riau as /kita/, in Palembang as /kito/, in Betawi and Perak as /kitɛ/ and in Kedah and Perlis as /kitɑ/.

Batavian and eastern dialects are sometimes regarded as Malay creole, because the speakers are not ethnically Malay.
